400	  assemble into a full symmetric \(\MonoidalCategory\) structure on
401	  \emph{all} of \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), discharging the pentagon,
402	  triangle and hexagon coherence identities. This is explicitly \emph{off-path}
403	  and \emph{not pursued}. One could in principle obtain it as the output of
404	  \(\mathtt{Localization.Monoidal.LocalizedMonoidal}\) applied to the
405	  already-monoidal presheaf category \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,\mathcal{O}_X\)
406	  and the sheafification localizer \(J.W\), supplying the instance
407	  \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) (\cref{lem:whisker_of_W}); but the group law never
408	  consumes a coherent monoidal category, so this construction is superseded and not
409	  carried out. The substrate provides only the \emph{existence} of the three
410	  coherence isomorphisms that the group law actually consumes.
411	
412	  Were that superseded route pursued, the instance \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\)
413	  would need \emph{no} closed structure: its
414	  \(\mathtt{whiskerLeft}\) field \(W\,g \Rightarrow W(F \triangleleft g)\) for an
415	  \emph{arbitrary} module \(F\) is the flatness-free stalkwise statement
416	  \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W} (a \(J.W\)-morphism \(g\) is a
417	  stalkwise isomorphism, so \((F \triangleleft g)_x = \mathrm{id}_{F_x} \otimes
418	  g_x\) is again an isomorphism for any \(F\)), the same technique Mathlib uses for
419	  fixed-base presheaves in
420	  \(\mathtt{CategoryTheory.Sites.Point.IsMonoidalW}\). It would use neither
421	  \(\mathtt{MonoidalClosed}(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R)\) nor the
422	  sectionwise-flatness hypothesis of \cref{lem:flat_whisker_localizer} (which is
423	  false over a non-affine open and is likewise off the critical path). The \emph{full}
424	  \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) instance and the \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) monoidal
425	  category are not on the critical path: the group law is realized without them. The
426	  single left-whisker existence lemma and its underlying d.1/d.2 stalk apparatus, by
427	  contrast, \emph{are} on the critical path, since the associator's sheafification
428	  transport consumes exactly that one whisker obligation
429	  (\cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whiskerleft_via_stalk}, closed via d.2).
430	
431	  Independently of how the monoidal structure is built, the relative Picard
432	  group law of \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid} is a structure on
433	  \emph{isomorphism classes} of line bundles, and every group axiom there is a
434	  \emph{proposition} of the form \(\mathrm{Nonempty}(\cdots \cong \cdots)\).
435	  Such a proposition is insensitive to which isomorphism witnesses it, so the
436	  coherence data of the monoidal category --- pentagon, triangle, hexagon, and
437	  a fortiori any monoidal-closed structure --- is never \emph{consumed} by the
438	  group law. The group law needs only the existence of the three coherence
439	  isomorphisms \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso,lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,%
440	  lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}, supplied directly: the associator by gluing canonical
441	  local isomorphisms through the restriction-compatibility isomorphism
442	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} (\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}), and the
443	  unitors and braiding by the cheap \(\mathtt{mapIso}\) pattern
444	  (\cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}). The carrier --- iso-classes
445	  of the substrate tensor \(\otimes_X\) --- is the scheme-theoretic analogue of
446	  Mathlib's Picard group \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\,R := \mathrm{Skeleton}(
447	  \mathtt{Module.Invertible}\,R)\) (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup}), but it
448	  is built \emph{differently}: Mathlib reads its \(\mathtt{CommGroup}\) off the
449	  \(\mathrm{Skeleton}\) of the coherent monoidal category
450	  \(\mathtt{MonoidalCategory}(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\,R)\) over a fixed ring,
451	  whereas here --- with no coherent monoidal category on
452	  \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) available for the varying structure sheaf --- the
453	  group is assembled by hand from the existence-of-isomorphism facts.
454	\end{remark}

456	\section{The lift through \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\)}
457	\label{sec:tensorobj_onproduct_lift}
458	
459	The consumer in chapter \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor} works not with
460	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}(C \times_k T)\) directly but with the subtype
461	\(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) of locally-trivial
462	modules on the fiber product \(C \times_k T\), introduced in
463	\cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback}. This section records the
464	\emph{existence-of-isomorphism facts on line bundles} (associativity, unit,
465	commutativity and inverse) that the relative Picard group law consumes. These facts
466	are supplied \emph{directly}: associativity by the direct-gluing associator
467	\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso} (through the linchpin
468	\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}), unit and commutativity by the cheap
469	\(\mathtt{mapIso}\) pattern (\cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}),
470	and the inverse existentially from \(\otimes\)-invertibility. They are \emph{not}
471	read off any \(\MonoidalCategory\) structure: the route that would derive them from
472	\cref{lem:jw_ismonoidal} (route~(e)) is superseded and off-path. The section also
473	records the closure of \(\otimes\) on the locally-trivial
474	\(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\) subtype (\cref{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}) and
475	the interaction with the pullback functor \(\pi_T^*\). The open-immersion
476	restriction-compatibility isomorphism \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} is on the
477	critical path precisely because it is consumed by the direct-gluing associator
478	\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}; it is \emph{not} tied to the superseded
479	whiskering-stability lemma \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}, which belongs
480	to the off-path route~(e).
481	
482	\begin{lemma}
483	
484	\leanok
485	[Sectionwise lax-monoidal structure on \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\,\varphi\)]
486	  \label{lem:restrictscalars_laxmonoidal}
487	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.restrictScalarsLaxMonoidal}
488	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
489	  % NOTE: this CommRingCat-level lax-monoidal instance
490	  % (`PresheafOfModules.restrictScalarsLaxMonoidal`, with helpers
491	  % `restrictScalarsLaxε`, `restrictScalarsLaxμ`) is a self-contained
492	  % supplement. It is NOT used by `lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso`, whose proof
493	  % (open-immersion sectionwise base change) identifies restriction along an
494	  % open immersion with base change along a structure-sheaf isomorphism and
495	  % needs no lax-monoidal pushforward. Retained for potential reuse; off the
496	  % critical path for the tensor group law.
497	  Let \(\varphi : R \to S\) be a morphism of presheaves of \emph{commutative}
498	  rings on a site (so that both \(R\) and \(S\) factor through
499	  \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\) and the category of presheaves of modules over each
500	  is monoidal). Then the presheaf-of-modules restriction-of-scalars functor
501	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.restrictScalars}\,\varphi\) is lax monoidal.
502	  Consequently, composing with the already-monoidal Mathlib functor
503	  \(\mathtt{pushforward}_0\mathtt{OfCommRingCat}\) via
504	  \(\mathtt{Functor.LaxMonoidal.comp}\), the full pushforward
505	  \[
506	    \mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi
507	      \;\;:=\;\;
508	    \mathtt{pushforward}_0\,F\,R \;\circ\; \mathtt{restrictScalars}\,\varphi
509	  \]
510	  is lax monoidal. This lemma is a self-contained
511	  \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\)-level lax-monoidal supplement: it stands on its own
512	  and is retained for potential reuse, but it is \emph{off the critical path}
513	  for the tensor group law. In particular it is \emph{not} an ingredient of
514	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}, whose proof identifies restriction along an
515	  open immersion with base change along a structure-sheaf isomorphism and
516	  consequently needs no lax-monoidal structure on the pushforward.
517	\end{lemma}
518	
519	\begin{proof}
520	
521	
522	\leanok
523	  This is a \emph{sectionwise} lift of the existing fact that, for a ring map
524	  \(f : R \to S\), the module restriction-of-scalars functor
525	  \(\mathtt{ModuleCat.restrictScalars}\,f\) is lax monoidal
526	  (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Monoidal.Adjunction}, where it is
527	  obtained as the \(\mathtt{rightAdjointLaxMonoidal}\) of the
528	  extension/restriction adjunction \(\mathtt{extendRestrictScalarsAdj}\) ---
529	  i.e.\ Mathlib already uses the same mate idiom one level down). The presheaf
530	  monoidal structure is computed sectionwise,
531	  \((M_1 \otimes M_2).\mathrm{obj}\,X = M_1.\mathrm{obj}\,X \otimes M_2.\mathrm{obj}\,X\),
532	  and the presheaf functor \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\,\varphi\) acts
533	  sectionwise as \(\mathtt{ModuleCat.restrictScalars}\,(\varphi.\mathrm{app}\,X)\).
534	  Hence the lax structure maps \(\mu\) (laxity) and \(\varepsilon\) (unit)
535	  assemble from the per-section \(\mathtt{ModuleCat}\) ones, the required
536	  naturality squares being the naturality of the sectionwise maps against the
537	  presheaf restriction morphisms. Packaging these as a
538	  \(\mathtt{Functor.CoreLaxMonoidal}\) yields the lax-monoidal structure; no
539	  new diagram chase is needed beyond transporting the sectionwise coherence.
540	  The composite \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi\) is then lax monoidal by
541	  \(\mathtt{Functor.LaxMonoidal.comp}\) together with the monoidality of
542	  \(\mathtt{pushforward}_0\mathtt{OfCommRingCat}\) (Mathlib), modulo a defeq
543	  transport across the definitional unfolding of \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi\).
544	
545	  The commutativity hypothesis is essential: the presheaf-of-modules monoidal
546	  category requires \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\)-valued presheaves of rings, so the
547	  instance must be stated over \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\)-factored \(R, S\). In
548	  the project this holds: the relevant \(\varphi\) is
549	  \((\mathtt{Scheme.Hom.toRingCatSheafHom}\,f).\mathrm{hom}\), which is
550	  structure-sheaf-valued and hence commutative-ring-valued.
551	\end{proof}
552	
553	\begin{lemma}
554	
555	
556	\leanok
557	[Tensor product commutes with restriction along an open immersion]
558	  \label{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}
559	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_restrict_iso}
560	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_tensorequiv}
561	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, let \(M, N \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) be
562	  \emph{arbitrary} \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules, and let
563	  \(f : U \hookrightarrow X\) be an open immersion, with \((-)|_f\) the
564	  restriction of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules along \(f\). The canonical comparison
565	  morphism
566	  \[
567	    (M \otimes_X N)\big|_f
568	      \;\xrightarrow{\;\;}\;
569	    M\big|_f \;\otimes_U\; N\big|_f
570	  \]
571	  is an isomorphism. No local-freeness, flatness, or line-bundle hypothesis on
572	  \(M, N\) is required: the statement holds for all \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules.
573	  The reason is structural to open immersions --- restriction along \(f\) is
574	  base change along the structure-sheaf \emph{isomorphism} induced by \(f\),
575	  not along a general ring map --- and base change along a ring isomorphism is
576	  trivially invertible and commutes with tensor products.
577	\end{lemma}
578	
579	\begin{proof}
580	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
581	  Write \(\varphi\) for the morphism of structure-sheaf-valued presheaves of
582	  rings induced by the open immersion \(f\) (concretely
583	  \(\varphi = (\mathtt{Scheme.Hom.toRingCatSheafHom}\,f).\mathrm{hom}\)), so that
584	  restriction along \(f\) is computed by the inverse-image (pullback) functor on
585	  presheaves of modules. The proof exploits the defining feature of an open
586	  immersion: the comparison of structure-sheaf rings over the image is an
587	  \emph{isomorphism}, not merely a ring map. For an open \(V \subseteq U\) the
588	  section ring \(\Gamma\bigl(X, f(V)\bigr)\) of \(\mathcal{O}_X\) over the image
589	  of \(V\) is identified with \(\Gamma(U, V)\) by the canonical \emph{ring
590	  isomorphism}
591	  \(f.\mathtt{appIso}\,V : \Gamma\bigl(X, f(V)\bigr) \xrightarrow{\sim}
592	  \Gamma(U, V)\)
593	  (the \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\) isomorphism
594	  \(\mathtt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Hom.appIso}\)). Restriction along \(f\) is
595	  therefore extension of scalars along
596	  this ring isomorphism, and the proof needs no flatness anywhere.
597	
598	  \emph{Step 1 (reduce restriction to the abstract pullback).} The restriction
599	  \((-)|_f\) of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules along the open immersion \(f\) is
600	  naturally isomorphic to the abstract presheaf-of-modules pullback
601	  \((\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}(-)\), via the
602	  present comparison \(\mathtt{Scheme.Modules.restrictFunctorIsoPullback}\). It
603	  therefore suffices to produce the comparison and prove it an isomorphism for
604	  the abstract pullback functor.
605	
606	  \emph{Step 2 (move the pullback inside the sheafification).} The substrate
607	  \(\otimes_X\) is sheafification of the presheaf-of-modules tensor product
608	  (\cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}). The pullback commutes with
609	  sheafification: there is a natural isomorphism
610	  \(\mathtt{sheafification} \circ \mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi
611	   \;\cong\;
612	   \mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullback}\,\varphi \circ \mathtt{sheafification}\)
613	  (the present \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.sheafificationCompPullback}\)). Hence the
614	  whole question descends to the presheaf level, where it is the base-change
615	  comparison
616	  \[
617	    (\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}(A \otimes B)
618	      \;\longrightarrow\;
619	    (\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}\,A
620	      \;\otimes\;
621	    (\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}\,B
622	  \]
623	  for the underlying presheaves of modules \(A, B\) of \(M\) and \(N\).
624	
625	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Modules on Ringed Spaces, Lemma
626	  % \ref{lemma-exactness-pushforward-pullback} (the pair $(f^*, f_*)$ of
627	  % pullback/pushforward of $\mathcal{O}$-modules along a morphism of ringed
628	  % spaces is an adjoint pair)
629	  % (read from references/stacks-modules.tex, L252-273).
630	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
631	  % "\begin{lemma}
632	  % \label{lemma-exactness-pushforward-pullback}
633	  % Let $f : (X, \mathcal{O}_X) \to (Y, \mathcal{O}_Y)$
634	  % be a morphism of ringed spaces.
635	  % \begin{enumerate}
636	  % \item The functor
637	  % $f_* : \textit{Mod}(\mathcal{O}_X) \to \textit{Mod}(\mathcal{O}_Y)$
638	  % is left exact. In fact it commutes with all limits.
639	  % \item The functor
640	  % $f^* : \textit{Mod}(\mathcal{O}_Y) \to \textit{Mod}(\mathcal{O}_X)$
641	  % is right exact. In fact it commutes with all colimits.
642	  %  [\dots]
643	  % \end{enumerate}
644	  % \end{lemma}
645	  %  [\dots]
646	  % Parts (1) and (2) hold because $(f^*, f_*)$ is an adjoint pair
647	  % of functors, see
648	  % Sheaves, Lemma \ref{sheaves-lemma-adjoint-pullback-pushforward-modules}"
649	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Modules on Ringed Spaces, Lemma
650	  \texttt{lemma-exactness-pushforward-pullback}: pullback and pushforward of
651	  $\mathcal{O}$-modules along a morphism form an adjoint pair $(f^*, f_*)$, the
652	  abstract universal property on which the comparison below rests.}
653	
654	  \emph{Step 3 (the genuine residual: H1 alone).} Completing this comparison at the
655	  presheaf level requires a sectionwise handle on the abstract functor
656	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullback}\,\varphi\), which is defined as the left
657	  adjoint of pushforward (the presheaf-of-modules avatar of the
658	  \(f^* \dashv f_*\) adjunction of the cited Stacks lemma) and admits no
659	  sectionwise formula in Mathlib at the pinned commit. The closure passes through
660	  two presheaf-level ingredients, of which exactly one now remains open.
661	
662	  The first ingredient --- a strong-monoidal upgrade of
663	  \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\) along the structure-sheaf ring \emph{isomorphism}
664	  \(f.\mathtt{appIso}\), so that base change along the isomorphism commutes with
665	  \(\otimes\) --- is \emph{closed in this chapter} as
666	  \cref{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_tensorequiv}
667	  (\(\mathtt{restrictScalarsRingIsoTensorEquiv}\)): for a ring isomorphism
668	  \(e : R \simeq S\) the canonical map \(a \otimes_R b \mapsto a \otimes_S b\) is an
669	  \(R\)-linear equivalence \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}_e(M) \otimes_R

674	  \[

676	  \]

678	  presheaf-level adjunction
679	  \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta \dashv \mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi\) and concludes
680	  that \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta\), being a left adjoint of
681	  \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi\), is canonically isomorphic to the abstract left
682	  adjoint \(\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi\). Here \(\beta\) is the structure map of the
683	  restriction functor, so that \((M|_f).\mathrm{val} =

685	  composing the resulting comparison with

689	  \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.pushforwardPushforwardAdj}\), and must be built from
690	  presheaf-level \(\mathtt{pushforwardNatTrans}\) and

702	  \((M \otimes_X N)|_f \cong M|_f \otimes_U N|_f\) on its sole consumer

873	  \label{lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial}

875	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
876	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, and let
877	  \(L, L' \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) be locally-trivial modules of
878	  rank one (i.e.\ line bundles). The tensor product \(L \otimes_X L'\) of
879	  \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj} is again locally trivial of rank
880	  one. Consequently the bifunctor \(\otimes_X\) restricts to a bifunctor

882	  of locally-trivial rank-one modules.

885	\begin{proof}
886	
887	  Pick a common affine open cover \(\{U_i\}\) of \(X\) on which both
888	  \(L|_{U_i}\) and \(L'|_{U_i}\) are free of rank one. On each \(U_i\)
889	  there are trivialisations \(L|_{U_i} \cong \mathcal{O}_{U_i}\) and
890	  \(L'|_{U_i} \cong \mathcal{O}_{U_i}\). The tensor product of two free
891	  rank-one modules over a commutative ring is again free of rank one;
892	  hence \((L \otimes_X L')|_{U_i} \cong \mathcal{O}_{U_i}\). So
893	  \(L \otimes_X L'\) is locally trivial of rank one, i.e.\ an object of
894	  \(\texttt{LineBundle}\,X\).

910	\begin{lemma}
911	% Closed, sorry-free standalone result (flat whiskering). Off the critical path:
912	% the associator is realized via the d.2 stalk-tensor route
913	% (lem:islocallyinjective_whiskerleft_via_stalk), not via this flat lemma.
914	
915	[Flat whiskering preserves the sheafification localizer]
916	  \label{lem:flat_whisker_localizer}
917	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
918	  Let \(R\) be a presheaf of commutative rings on a site carrying a Grothendieck
919	  topology \(J\), and let \(J.W\) be the class of morphisms of presheaves of
920	  \(R\)-modules that are inverted by sheafification --- equivalently, the
921	  \emph{locally bijective} morphisms, i.e.\ those that are both locally injective
922	  and locally surjective for \(J\) (the characterisation
923	  \(\mathtt{WEqualsLocallyBijective}\)). Let \(F\) be a presheaf of \(R\)-modules
924	  that is \emph{flat} (sectionwise: \(F(U)\) is a flat \(R(U)\)-module for every
925	  object \(U\) of the site). Then for every morphism \(g\) in \(J.W\) the
926	  left-whiskered morphism
927	  \[
928	    F \triangleleft g \;\;=\;\; \mathrm{id}_F \otimes g
929	  \]
930	  (the action of \(F \otimes^{p} -\) on \(g\), with \(\otimes^{p}\) the
931	  presheaf-of-modules tensor product) again lies in \(J.W\). By the symmetry of
932	  the presheaf-of-modules monoidal structure, the right-whiskered morphism
933	  \(g \triangleright F\) likewise lies in \(J.W\) when \(F\) is flat.
934	
935	  Both halves --- the left-whiskering statement and its braiding-conjugate
936	  right-whiskering statement --- are established. They are valid standalone
937	  results about flat whiskering of sheafification-localizer morphisms. They are,
938	  however, \emph{off the critical path}: the live whiskering-stability obligation
939	  for the unconditional associator is closed for an \emph{arbitrary} whiskering
940	  object \(F\) by the stalk--tensor commutation
941	  \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whiskerleft_via_stalk}
942	  (\cref{sec:tensorobj_stalk_tensor}), which never invokes the
943	  sectionwise-flatness hypothesis of the present lemma. The sectionwise flatness
944	  condition --- \(F(U)\) flat over \(R(U)\) for \emph{every} object \(U\) of the
945	  site, including non-affine opens --- is not supplied by \(\otimes\)-invertibility
946	  or local triviality, which are affine-local properties; the present (flat) lemma
947	  is therefore retained as an independent, closed result, superseded on the critical
948	  path by the d.2 route of \cref{sec:tensorobj_stalk_tensor}.
949	\end{lemma}
950	
951	\begin{proof}
952	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
953	  By the locally-bijective characterisation of the sheafification localizer
954	  (\(J.W\) is exactly the class of morphisms that are \(J\)-locally injective and
955	  \(J\)-locally surjective), it suffices to show that \(F \triangleleft g\) is
956	  both locally injective and locally surjective whenever \(g\) is.
957	
958	  The presheaf-of-modules tensor product is computed sectionwise,
959	  \((F \otimes^{p} G)(U) = F(U) \otimes_{R(U)} G(U)\), so for
960	  \(g : A \to B\) the whiskered morphism acts on sections over \(U\) as the
961	  \(R(U)\)-linear map
962	  \[
963	    \mathrm{id}_{F(U)} \otimes g(U) \;\colon\;
964	      F(U) \otimes_{R(U)} A(U) \;\longrightarrow\; F(U) \otimes_{R(U)} B(U).
965	  \]
966	
967	  \emph{Local surjectivity} is automatic and needs no hypothesis on \(F\):
968	  tensoring is right exact, so applying \(F \otimes^{p} -\) carries the cokernel
969	  of \(g\) to the cokernel of \(F \triangleleft g\); since \(g\) is locally
970	  surjective its cokernel is locally zero, hence so is the cokernel of
971	  \(F \triangleleft g\), and \(F \triangleleft g\) is locally surjective.
972	
973	  \emph{Local injectivity} is where flatness enters. Because each \(F(U)\) is a
974	  flat \(R(U)\)-module, the functor \(F(U) \otimes_{R(U)} -\) preserves injective
975	  linear maps (Mathlib
976	  \(\mathtt{Module.Flat.lTensor\_preserves\_injective\_linearMap}\)). Thus
977	  whenever \(g(U)\) is injective on a covering sieve, so is
978	  \((\mathrm{id}_{F(U)} \otimes g(U))\); the local injectivity of \(g\) is
979	  therefore transported sectionwise to \(F \triangleleft g\).
980	
981	  Locally injective and locally surjective together give
982	  \(F \triangleleft g \in J.W\). The right-whiskered statement
983	  \(g \triangleright F\) follows by conjugating \(F \triangleleft g\) with the
984	  braiding of the symmetric presheaf-of-modules monoidal structure, which is an
985	  isomorphism and hence preserves membership in \(J.W\); the flatness of \(F\) is
986	  the only input, and it is symmetric in the two tensor factors.
987	\end{proof}

1048	\section{Route~(e): the \(J.W\)-monoidality instance and the localized monoidal structure}
1049	\label{sec:tensorobj_route_e}
1050	
1051	\emph{Full-monoidal-category construction --- off path, not to be formalized.} The
1052	construction below realizes a \emph{full} symmetric monoidal category on all of
1053	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) by instantiating Mathlib's
1054	localization-of-monoidal-categories API. It was the original plan for supplying the
1055	associator. The relative Picard group law, however, is a structure on
1056	\emph{isomorphism classes} of invertible sheaves and consumes only the
1057	\emph{existence} of the associator, unitor and braiding isomorphisms
1058	(\cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup}, built by hand as the scheme-theoretic
1059	analogue of Mathlib's \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\), not off a coherent monoidal
1060	category's \(\mathtt{Skeleton}\)). The associator is obtained as the sheafification
1061	transport of the presheaf associator (\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}), whose
1062	\emph{single} open left-whisker obligation is closed by the d.2 stalk--tensor
1063	commutation (\cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whiskerleft_via_stalk,%
1064	lem:stalk_tensor_commutation}); it uses \emph{no} \emph{full}
1065	\((J.W).\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) instance and \emph{no} \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\)
1066	monoidal category. Accordingly the \emph{full monoidal-category} target of this
1067	section --- the \emph{pair} of whiskering-stability fields (\cref{lem:whisker_of_W})
1068	and the \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) instance (\cref{lem:jw_ismonoidal}) --- is off
1069	path and must not be re-attempted; it is kept for the historical record. The d.1/d.2
1070	stalk apparatus it shares with the live single whisker obligation is \emph{not}
1071	vestigial, however: it is on the critical path through
1072	\cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whiskerleft_via_stalk}.
1073	
1074	The monoidal structure on the substrate is realized by \emph{instantiating}
1075	Mathlib's localization-of-monoidal-categories API
1076	(\cref{sec:tensorobj_api_survey}): apply
1077	\(\mathtt{Localization.Monoidal.LocalizedMonoidal}\) to the already-monoidal
1078	presheaf category \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,\mathcal{O}_X\) and the
1079	sheafification localizer \(J.W\). The single new input the API requires is the
1080	instance \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\), whose two fields are the
1081	whiskering-stability lemmas below.
1082	
1083	\paragraph{What the group law consumes.} The group law is assembled directly on
1084	isomorphism classes of \emph{locally trivial} (invertible) sheaves
1085	(\cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup}), mirroring Mathlib's ring-level Picard
1086	group \(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\) / \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\)
1087	(\texttt{Mathlib/RingTheory/PicardGroup.lean}). It needs only the \emph{existence} of
1088	the associator \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}, the unitors \cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso}
1089	and the braiding \cref{lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}, together with \(\otimes\)-invertibility
1090	supplying inverses; it uses \emph{no} \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) instance, \emph{no}
1091	\(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) monoidal category, and \emph{no} \(\mathtt{Skeleton}\).
1092	It does, however, consume the associator \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}, which is
1093	realized as the sheafification transport of the presheaf associator and whose
1094	\emph{single} open left-whisker obligation \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whiskerleft_via_stalk}
1095	is closed by the stalk--tensor commutation (d.2, \cref{lem:stalk_tensor_commutation}).
1096	Hence the stalk ingredients (d.1)/(d.2) are \emph{on} the critical path of the group
1097	law --- through the associator's single whisker obligation --- even though the full
1098	\(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal} \to \mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) monoidal category on all of
1099	\(\mathtt{SheafOfModules}\) (the subject of the present section) is \emph{not} built:
1100	that full monoidal generality, with its \emph{pair} of whiskering-stability fields
1101	(\cref{lem:whisker_of_W}) and all derived coherence, is what the group law does not
1102	consume. The single open obligation of the present section's full-monoidal target is
1103	thus the local-injectivity half packaged for an arbitrary whisker object
1104	(\cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}), itself a consequence of the same d.2
1105	commutation.
1106	
1107	The following lemma is the \(R_x\)-linear stalk-map packaging (the ``d.1''
1108	ingredient). It is consumed by the stalk--tensor commutation of
1109	\cref{sec:tensorobj_stalk_tensor} (\cref{lem:stalk_tensor_commutation,
1110	lem:islocallyinjective_whiskerleft_via_stalk}) and is therefore a live ingredient
1111	of the unconditional associator route.

1113	\begin{lemma}
1114	% Live ingredient: consumed by lem:stalk_tensor_commutation and
1115	% lem:islocallyinjective_whiskerleft_via_stalk (sec:tensorobj_stalk_tensor).
1116	
1117	[The $R.\mathtt{stalk}\,x$-linear stalk map of a morphism of presheaves of modules]
1118	  \label{lem:stalk_linear_map}
1119	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
1120	  Let \(X\) be a topological space, \(R\) a presheaf of commutative rings on
1121	  \(X\), and \(x \in X\) a point. For a morphism \(g : M \to N\) of presheaves of
1122	  \(R\)-modules, the induced map on stalks
1123	  \[
1124	    g_x \;\colon\; M_x \;\longrightarrow\; N_x
1125	  \]
1126	  is \(R.\mathtt{stalk}\,x\)-linear (\(\mathtt{stalkLinearMap}\)), and is
1127	  characterised on germs by
1128	  \(g_x(\mathrm{germ}_x\,s) = \mathrm{germ}_x(g_U\,s)\)
1129	  (\(\mathtt{stalkLinearMap\_germ}\)). When the underlying map of
1130	  \(\mathtt{Ab}\)-valued stalks is an isomorphism, \(g_x\) is bijective
1131	  (\(\mathtt{stalkLinearMap\_bijective\_of\_isIso}\)); in that case it bundles to
1132	  an \(R.\mathtt{stalk}\,x\)-linear equivalence
1133	  \(M_x \xrightarrow{\sim} N_x\) (\(\mathtt{stalkLinearEquivOfIsIso}\)).
1134	
1135	  These four declarations are the \emph{(d.1)-partial implementation} --- the
1136	  \(R_x\)-linearity packaging of stalk maps --- consumed by the
1137	  \(\mathrm{id} \otimes g_x\) step in the proofs of
1138	  \cref{lem:stalk_tensor_commutation} and
1139	  \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whiskerleft_via_stalk}. They build on the
1140	  stalk module structure of \texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Stalk};
1141	  the linearity packaging itself is project-original.
1142	\end{lemma}
1143	
1144	\begin{proof}
1145	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
1146	  The stalk \(M_x = \mathtt{stalk}\,M.\mathtt{presheaf}\,x\) carries its
1147	  \(R.\mathtt{stalk}\,x\)-module structure from
1148	  \texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Stalk}. The underlying additive map
1149	  \(g_x\) on stalks is the filtered colimit of the section maps \(g_U\); since
1150	  each section map is \(R(U)\)-linear and the scalar action on the stalk is the
1151	  colimit of the section actions, \(g_x\) respects the
1152	  \(R.\mathtt{stalk}\,x\)-action, which gives the linear map together with its
1153	  germ characterisation. Bijectivity is read off the underlying
1154	  \(\mathtt{Ab}\)-stalk isomorphism (a bijective linear map is a linear
1155	  equivalence), and bundling the inverse yields the linear equivalence.
1156	\end{proof}

1176	[Local injectivity of a whiskered localizer morphism (stalkwise, flatness-free)]
1177	  \label{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}
1178	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
1179	  Let \(R\) be a presheaf of commutative rings on a site carrying a Grothendieck
1180	  topology \(J\), and let \(J.W\) be the sheafification localizer (the locally
1181	  bijective morphisms). Concretely the site carries the
1182	  \(\mathtt{WEqualsLocallyBijective}\) property for \(\mathtt{Ab}\), so \(J.W\) is
1183	  exactly the class of \(J\)-locally bijective morphisms; this is recorded as a
1184	  typeclass hypothesis \([J.\mathtt{WEqualsLocallyBijective}\,\mathtt{Ab}]\) on
1185	  the statement. Let \(F\) be an \emph{arbitrary} presheaf of
1186	  \(R\)-modules, and let \(g : M \to N\) be a morphism of presheaves of
1187	  \(R\)-modules whose underlying morphism of presheaves of abelian groups
1188	  \((\mathtt{toPresheaf}\,R).\mathrm{map}\,g\) lies in \(J.W\). Then the
1189	  left-whiskered morphism
1190	  \[
1191	    F \triangleleft g \;\;=\;\; \mathrm{id}_F \otimes g
1192	  \]
1193	  is \emph{\(J\)-locally injective}. No flatness and no local-triviality
1194	  hypothesis on \(F\) is required.
1195	
1196	  This is the \(\mathtt{whiskerLeft}\)-field content (the injectivity half) of the
1197	  \emph{full} instance \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) (\cref{lem:jw_ismonoidal}), the
1198	  load-bearing field of the off-path \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) monoidal-category
1199	  path (arbitrary \(F\)). Its content for an arbitrary \(F\) is exactly the live lemma
1200	  \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whiskerleft_via_stalk}, which the associator's
1201	  sheafification transport consumes and which is closed \emph{unconditionally} by the
1202	  d.2 stalk--tensor commutation (\cref{lem:stalk_tensor_commutation}); the d.1/d.2
1203	  ingredients are therefore on the critical path. The two proofs recorded below --- a
1204	  sheaf-level argument specialised to locally trivial \(F\), and the general stalkwise
1205	  argument via (d.1)/(d.2) --- are retained for the historical record; the live group
1206	  law closes this obligation in its general (d.2) form through
1207	  \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whiskerleft_via_stalk}.
1208	\end{lemma}

1210	\begin{proof}
1211	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:stalk_linear_map,
1212	        lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso, lem:tensorobj_unit_iso}
1213	
1214	  \emph{PRIMARY route (\(F\) locally trivial --- the consumer's case; sheaf-level,
1215	  no stalks, no (d.2)).} Suppose \(F\) is locally trivial
1216	  (\(\mathtt{LineBundle.IsLocallyTrivial}\,F\)), which is exactly the hypothesis its
1217	  sole consumer --- the associator \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso} --- supplies.
1218	  \(J\)-local injectivity of a morphism is \emph{local on a covering family}: it
1219	  suffices to produce a cover of \(X\) on each member of which \(F \triangleleft g\)
1220	  restricts to a locally injective morphism. Take a trivialising open cover
1221	  \(\{V\}\) of \(X\) for \(F\), so that \(F|_V \cong \mathcal{O}_V\) on each \(V\).
1222	  On such a \(V\):
1223	  \begin{itemize}
1224	    \item the substrate--restriction compatibility \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}
1225	      gives a canonical isomorphism
1226	      \[
1227	        (F \triangleleft g)\big|_V \;\cong\; (F|_V) \triangleleft (g|_V)
1228	      \]
1229	      --- restriction along the open immersion \(V \hookrightarrow X\) commutes with
1230	      \(\otimes\), and the whiskering \(F \triangleleft g = \mathrm{id}_F \otimes g\)
1231	      is \(\otimes\) with an identity, so it restricts to \(\mathrm{id}_{F|_V}
1232	      \otimes (g|_V) = (F|_V) \triangleleft (g|_V)\);
1233	    \item the trivialisation \(F|_V \cong \mathcal{O}_V\) together with the left
1234	      unitor \cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso} (\(\mathcal{O}_V \otimes_V (-) \cong
1235	      (-)\)) identifies \((F|_V) \triangleleft (g|_V) \cong g|_V\).
1236	  \end{itemize}
1237	  Composing, \((F \triangleleft g)|_V\) is isomorphic to \(g|_V\). Now \(g \in J.W\),
1238	  and membership in \(J.W\) (local bijectivity) is stable under restriction along an
1239	  open immersion, so \(g|_V \in J.W\); in particular \(g|_V\) is \(J\)-locally
1240	  injective. An isomorphism preserves local injectivity, hence \((F \triangleleft
1241	  g)|_V\) is locally injective on \(V\). As the \(\{V\}\) cover \(X\) and local
1242	  injectivity is local on the cover, \(F \triangleleft g\) is \(J\)-locally
1243	  injective. \emph{No stalks and no (d.2) stalk--tensor commutation enter}; the
1244	  single comparison lemma \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} (a presheaf-level
1245	  comparison, decomposed into its two open-immersion base-change halves) carries the
1246	  argument, and thereby moves \emph{onto} the critical path.
1247	
1248	  This is \emph{not} the section-level route that tensors a bare section map \(g(V)\)
1249	  --- only injective --- and stalls on a \(\mathrm{Tor}_1\)/flatness obligation. Here
1250	  the argument is sheaf-level and uses that \(g\) is locally \emph{bijective} (a
1251	  \(J.W\) morphism), reduced through the unitor to \(g|_V\) (again a \(J.W\)
1252	  morphism); a mere injection is never tensored, so no flatness is needed.
1253	
1254	  \emph{FALLBACK route (arbitrary \(F\); stalkwise, required only for the
1255	  full-generality route~(e) \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) path).} For an
1256	  \emph{arbitrary} (not necessarily locally trivial) \(F\), local injectivity is
1257	  proved stalkwise, by the argument Mathlib uses for fixed-base presheaves in
1258	  \(\mathtt{CategoryTheory.Sites.Point.IsMonoidalW}\)
1259	  (\cref{sec:tensorobj_api_survey}), ported to \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\). A
1260	  morphism in \(J.W\) is, on the topological site of \(X\), a \emph{stalkwise
1261	  isomorphism}: at each point \(x\) the induced map on stalks \(g_x\) is an
1262	  isomorphism. The presheaf-of-modules tensor is sectionwise, so it commutes with
1263	  the stalk (a filtered colimit), giving
1264	  \[
1265	    (F \triangleleft g)_x
1266	      \;=\; \mathrm{id}_{F_x} \otimes_{R_x} g_x
1267	      \;\colon\; F_x \otimes_{R_x} M_x \;\longrightarrow\; F_x \otimes_{R_x} N_x .
1268	  \]
1269	  Tensoring an isomorphism \(g_x\) by the identity \(\mathrm{id}_{F_x}\) yields an
1270	  isomorphism --- no flatness is needed, since \(g_x\) is invertible, not merely
1271	  injective. Hence \(F \triangleleft g\) is a stalkwise isomorphism, in particular
1272	  locally bijective, in particular locally injective, for \emph{any} \(F\).

2564	\section{Pullback-monoidality: invertibility under a general scheme morphism}
2565	\label{sec:tensorobj_pullback_monoidality}
2566	
2567	The relative Picard consumer (\cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor}) re-bases its
2568	line-bundle carrier onto the \(\otimes\)-invertibility predicate
2569	\(\mathtt{IsInvertible}\) of \cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}, and its
2570	functorial structure maps are \emph{pullback} maps of \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules: the
2571	projection pullback \(\pi_T^*\) along \(C \times_S T \to T\) and the base-change
2572	pullback along \(C \times_S T' \to C \times_S T\). For these maps to descend to the
2573	\(\mathtt{IsInvertible}\) carrier one needs that \emph{pullback preserves
2574	\(\otimes\)-invertibility} for \emph{general} morphisms of schemes --- the
2575	projection and the base-change morphism are neither open immersions nor flat. This
2576	is the target lemma \cref{lem:isinvertible_pullback} of the section.
2577	
2578	This is a genuinely different fact from the open-immersion compatibility
2579	\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}, and not a corollary of it. That lemma handles
2580	restriction along an open immersion, and it does so by routing through the
2581	\emph{lax right} adjoint \(\mathtt{pushforward}/\mathtt{restrictScalars}\), which is
2582	strong monoidal there \emph{only} because an open immersion's structure-sheaf ring
2583	map is a local \emph{isomorphism} (\(f.\mathtt{appIso}\)). A general scheme
2584	morphism has no such isomorphism, so the right-adjoint route is unavailable.
2585	
2586	\paragraph{The general comparison is descoped.} One might attempt
2587	\cref{lem:isinvertible_pullback} through the \emph{general} pullback--tensor
2588	comparison \(f^*(M \otimes_X N) \cong f^*M \otimes_Y f^*N\) for \emph{arbitrary}
2589	\(M, N\) (\cref{lem:pullback_tensor_iso}). Mathematically that isomorphism is true
2590	for every morphism --- pullback is extension of scalars and extension of scalars is
2591	strong monoidal --- but \emph{formalising the general iso is Mathlib-scale and is not
2592	needed on any project path}. The obstruction is structural: the project carries
2593	\(f^*\) \emph{abstractly}, as the left adjoint
2594	\((\mathtt{pushforward}\,f).\mathtt{leftAdjoint}\), with no sectionwise value to which
2595	one could attach the extension-of-scalars tensorator. Building the comparison as an
2596	isomorphism by the concrete-model route would require a concrete model \(P =
2597	\mathtt{sheafification} \circ (\text{presheaf base change})\), and that model needs
2598	two ingredients absent from Mathlib at the pinned commit: a presheaf-level
2599	extension-of-scalars functor \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.extendScalars}\), and a
2600	topological inverse-image functor (a left Kan extension along
2601	\((\mathtt{Opens.map}\,f.\mathtt{base}).\mathrm{op}\)). The Stacks proof of the
2602	general statement is itself recorded as ``Omitted''
2603	(\cref{lem:pullback_tensor_iso}): it is standard mathematics with no slick
2604	formalisation. We therefore \emph{descope} the general iso and prove only the
2605	invertible case, which is all the downstream consumers (relative Picard functor,
2606	Quot embedding, tangent computation, Albanese universal property) ever require.
2607	
2608	\paragraph{The route: local trivialisation of the invertible pair.} For invertible
2609	\(M, N\) the comparison map \(\delta_{\mathrm{sheaf}} : f^*(M \otimes_X N) \to f^*M
2610	\otimes_Y f^*N\) (\cref{lem:pullback_tensor_map}) \emph{is} an isomorphism, and we
2611	prove this not in general but on the specific invertible pair, by local
2612	trivialisation. The comparison map itself exists for arbitrary \(M, N\): it is the
2613	sheaf-level transport of the presheaf-level oplax comparison
2614	(\cref{lem:presheaf_pullback_oplaxmonoidal}) through sheafification, and it sidesteps
2615	the absent inverse image because it is only a \emph{map}, not an iso. Invertibility
2616	enters only to upgrade that map to an iso: an invertible module is locally free of
2617	rank \(1\) (\cref{lem:isinvertible_implies_locallytrivial}), so \(M, N\), and hence
2618	\(M \otimes_X N\), become \(\mathcal{O}_X\) on a common affine cover; their pullbacks
2619	become \(\mathcal{O}_Y\) on the preimage cover (\(\mathtt{IsLocallyTrivial.pullback}\)),
2620	where \(\delta_{\mathrm{sheaf}}\) restricts to the canonical comparison
2621	\(\mathcal{O} \otimes \mathcal{O} \cong \mathcal{O}\), an isomorphism; and a morphism
2622	of \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules that is an isomorphism after restriction to every member
2623	of a cover is an isomorphism (\cref{lem:isiso_of_isiso_restrict}).
2624	
2625	\paragraph{Two negative results that pin the route.} First, one might hope that since
2626	pullback is \emph{some} (op)lax monoidal functor, invertible objects are preserved for
2627	free. This is \emph{false}: global sections \(\Gamma\) is lax monoidal, yet
2628	\(\Gamma(\mathbb{P}^1, \mathcal{O}(1)) = 0\) is not an invertible module, so a mere
2629	oplax comparison map does \emph{not} suffice --- one genuinely has to prove the tensor
2630	comparison is an \emph{isomorphism}. This is exactly why
2631	\cref{lem:isinvertible_pullback} cannot be read off the presheaf-level oplax structure
2632	of \cref{lem:presheaf_pullback_oplaxmonoidal} alone, and must localise.
2633	
2634	Second --- and crucially --- the local-trivialisation route is \emph{not} the shelved
2635	``invertible \(\Rightarrow\) locally \(\mathcal{O}\) \(\Rightarrow\) recover
2636	invertibility from local triviality'' mirage. That mirage is the \emph{reverse} bridge
2637	\(\mathtt{IsLocallyTrivial} \Rightarrow \mathtt{IsInvertible}\), the dual/tensor-inverse
2638	manufacture that the invertibility-carrier pivot (\cref{sec:tensorobj_pic_carrier}) was
2639	built to delete; it stays off-path here. Our route never recovers invertibility from
2640	local triviality: it produces the explicit witness \(f^*N\) directly and uses local
2641	triviality \emph{only} in the \emph{forward} direction
2642	\(\mathtt{IsInvertible} \Rightarrow \mathtt{IsLocallyTrivial}\)
2643	(\cref{lem:isinvertible_implies_locallytrivial}, invertible \(\Rightarrow\) locally free
2644	of rank \(1\), which is genuinely available), and only to certify that the comparison
2645	map \(\delta_{\mathrm{sheaf}}\) is an isomorphism on that one pair.
2646	
2647	\paragraph{Guardrail.} The iso-ness of \(\delta_{\mathrm{sheaf}}\) must be checked
2648	\emph{on a cover} via \cref{lem:isiso_of_isiso_restrict}, \emph{not} stalkwise.
2649	Reducing it to stalks would re-derive the varying-ring stalk-tensor commutation; the cover route avoids that
2650	entirely, because on each trivialising open all three modules are literally
2651	\(\mathcal{O}\) and the comparison is the canonical \(\mathcal{O} \otimes \mathcal{O}
2652	\cong \mathcal{O}\).
2653	
2654	\begin{lemma}
2655	[The presheaf pushforward is lax monoidal]
2656	  \label{lem:presheaf_pushforward_laxmonoidal}
2657	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.presheafPushforwardLaxMonoidal}
2658	  Let \(\varphi\) be a morphism of \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\)-valued ring presheaves,
2659	  presenting a pushforward of presheaves of modules. Then the presheaf pushforward
2660	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pushforward}\,\varphi\) is \emph{lax monoidal}: it
2661	  carries the comparison
2662	  \[
2663	    \mu :
2664	      (\mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}\,A
2665	        \otimes (\mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}\,B
2666	      \;\longrightarrow\;
2667	      (\mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}\,(A \otimes B),
2668	  \]
2669	  obtained as the composite of the strong-monoidal pushforward of presheaves over a
2670	  fixed ring presheaf (\(\mathtt{pushforward}_0\mathtt{OfCommRingCat}\)) with the lax
2671	  monoidal restriction of scalars \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\,\varphi\) along the
2672	  ring-presheaf map. This is a statement at the \emph{presheaf} level of
2673	  \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules; it does not require a monoidal structure on
2674	  \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules}\).
2675	\end{lemma}
2676	
2677	\begin{proof}
2678	  Restriction of scalars along a homomorphism of ring presheaves is lax monoidal --- the
2679	  comparison sends \((a \otimes b)\) to itself under the identification of the
2680	  restricted tensor product with the source tensor product --- and the pushforward of
2681	  presheaves of modules over a \emph{fixed} ring presheaf is strong monoidal. The
2682	  presheaf pushforward along \(\varphi\) factors as the composite of these two functors,
2683	  so its lax monoidal structure is the composite of the strong-monoidal structure on the
2684	  first factor with the lax structure on the second; the comparison \(\mu\) is the
2685	  whiskered composite of the two comparisons.
2686	\end{proof}
2687	
2688	\begin{lemma}
2689	[The presheaf pullback is oplax monoidal; the comparison map $\delta$]
2690	  \label{lem:presheaf_pullback_oplaxmonoidal}
2691	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.presheafPullbackOplaxMonoidal}
2692	  \uses{lem:presheaf_pushforward_laxmonoidal}
2693	  In the same setting, the presheaf pullback \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullback}\,\varphi\)
2694	  --- the left adjoint of the lax-monoidal presheaf pushforward of
2695	  \cref{lem:presheaf_pushforward_laxmonoidal} --- is \emph{oplax monoidal}. In
2696	  particular it carries the canonical comparison \emph{morphism}
2697	  \[
2698	    \delta :
2699	      (\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}\,(A \otimes B)
2700	      \;\longrightarrow\;
2701	      (\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}\,A
2702	        \otimes (\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}\,B,
2703	  \]
2704	  produced by the doctrinal adjunction transfer
2705	  \(\mathtt{Adjunction.leftAdjointOplaxMonoidal}\): the oplax structure on a left
2706	  adjoint is the adjunction mate of the lax structure on its right adjoint. The
2707	  comparison \(\delta\) is at the \emph{presheaf} level, and it is only a
2708	  \emph{morphism}: upgrading \(\delta\) to an isomorphism is the remaining content, and
2709	  is exactly what the local-trivialisation argument of \cref{lem:isinvertible_pullback}
2710	  supplies for an invertible pair.
2711	\end{lemma}
2712	
2713	\begin{proof}
2714	  \uses{lem:presheaf_pushforward_laxmonoidal}
2715	  The presheaf pullback is by construction the left adjoint of the presheaf pushforward,
2716	  which is lax monoidal by \cref{lem:presheaf_pushforward_laxmonoidal}. Mathlib's
2717	  \(\mathtt{Adjunction.leftAdjointOplaxMonoidal}\) turns the lax-monoidal structure on a
2718	  right adjoint into an oplax-monoidal structure on the left adjoint, with the oplax
2719	  comparison \(\delta\) defined as the mate of the lax comparison \(\mu\) under the
2720	  adjunction. Applying this to the pullback--pushforward adjunction yields the asserted
2721	  oplax structure and the comparison morphism \(\delta\).
2722	\end{proof}

2724	\begin{lemma}
2725	[Pullback commutes with $\otimes$ for a general scheme morphism --- descoped]
2726	  \label{lem:pullback_tensor_iso}
2727	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}
2728	  Let \(f : Y \to X\) be a morphism of schemes and let
2729	  \(M, N \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) be \emph{arbitrary} \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules.
2730	  Writing \(f^* = \mathtt{Scheme.Modules.pullback}\,f\) for the inverse-image functor
2731	  of \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules, the canonical comparison
2732	  \[
2733	    f^*(M \otimes_X N)
2734	      \;\xrightarrow{\;\sim\;}\;
2735	    (f^*M) \otimes_Y (f^*N)
2736	  \]
2737	  is an isomorphism, natural in \(M\) and \(N\). No local-freeness, flatness, or
2738	  open-immersion hypothesis is required: it holds for all \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules
2739	  and all morphisms \(f\).
2740	
2741	  \emph{This general isomorphism is descoped and is not formalised: it is off the
2742	  critical path.} It is confirmed Mathlib-scale --- the concrete-model route below needs
2743	  a presheaf-level extension-of-scalars functor \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.extendScalars}\)
2744	  and a topological inverse-image left Kan extension, both absent from Mathlib at the
2745	  pinned commit, and the Stacks proof is recorded merely as ``Omitted'' --- and it is not
2746	  needed on any project path, since every consumer pulls back only \emph{invertible}
2747	  modules. The substrate obtains \cref{lem:isinvertible_pullback} through the
2748	  local-trivialisation route instead, which never builds this general comparison; the
2749	  block is retained only to document the general fact and why it is hard. Accordingly no
2750	  Lean declaration is associated with this statement.

2765	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Modules on Ringed Spaces, Lemma
2766	  \texttt{lemma-tensor-product-pullback}: for a morphism of ringed spaces
2767	  \(f : (X, \mathcal{O}_X) \to (Y, \mathcal{O}_Y)\) and \(\mathcal{O}_Y\)-modules
2768	  \(\mathcal{F}, \mathcal{G}\), one has \(f^*(\mathcal{F} \otimes \mathcal{G}) =
2769	  f^*\mathcal{F} \otimes f^*\mathcal{G}\) functorially.}

2772	\begin{proof}
2773	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso,
2774	        lem:presheaf_pullback_oplaxmonoidal}
2775	  We record the would-be concrete-model proof to document where it stalls; the statement
2776	  is descoped and this argument is \emph{not} carried out in the formalisation. Read it as
2777	  an explanation of why the general iso is Mathlib-scale.
2778	
2779	  Mathematically, pullback is extension of scalars and extension of scalars is strong
2780	  monoidal, so the comparison is an isomorphism for every \(f\). The only work is to
2781	  carry this through the formalisation's \emph{abstract} pullback \(f^* =
2782	  (\mathtt{pushforward}\,f).\mathtt{leftAdjoint}\), which has no sectionwise value. We
2783	  do not compute \(f^*\) sectionwise; instead we equip a \emph{concrete} model of the
2784	  pullback with a strong-monoidal structure and transport it to \(f^*\) along the
2785	  uniqueness of left adjoints. This mirrors Mathlib's own construction of the
2786	  extension-of-scalars monoidal structure in
2787	  \texttt{Mathlib/Algebra/Category/ModuleCat/Monoidal/Adjunction.lean}, where the
2788	  \emph{left} adjoint's strong-monoidal structure is built explicitly (via the
2789	  base-change distributivity isomorphism) and only afterwards is the right adjoint's
2790	  lax structure derived from the adjunction --- never the reverse direction. The
2791	  argument has three steps.
2792	
2793	  \emph{Step 1 (a concrete strong-monoidal pullback \(P\)).} Build the concrete functor
2794	  \[
2795	    P \;=\; \mathtt{sheafification} \;\circ\; (\text{presheaf base change}),
2796	  \]
2797	  the presheaf-level inverse image (sectionwise extension of scalars along the
2798	  structure-sheaf ring maps of \(f\)) followed by sheafification on the small Zariski
2799	  site --- the same \(\mathtt{sheafificationCompPullback}\) shape already used in
2800	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}. Equip \(P\) with a strong-monoidal structure. Its
2801	  tensorator is the linear-algebra strong-monoidal core
2802	  \(\mathtt{TensorProduct.AlgebraTensorModule.distribBaseChange}\),
2803	  \[
2804	    (M \otimes_R N) \otimes_R S
2805	      \;\cong\;
2806	    (M \otimes_R S) \otimes_S (N \otimes_R S),
2807	  \]
2808	  an isomorphism for \emph{every} ring map \(R \to S\), with no flatness, sheafified
2809	  pointwise; this is precisely Mathlib's \((\mathtt{extendScalars}\,\varphi).\mathtt{Monoidal}\)
2810	  lifted to sheaves. The unit comparison is the already-landed sheafification-monoidality
2811	  brick \(\mathtt{sheafifyTensorUnitIso}\). Thus \(P\) is strong monoidal with each
2812	  tensorator and the unit comparison an isomorphism, with no hypothesis on \(f\).
2813	
2814	  \emph{Step 2 (\(P\) is left adjoint to pushforward).} Exhibit an adjunction
2815	  \(P \dashv \mathtt{SheafOfModules.pushforward}\,f\). This is the universal property
2816	  of sheafified extension of scalars: the presheaf base change is left adjoint to
2817	  presheaf restriction of scalars, and sheafification is left adjoint to the inclusion,
2818	  so the composite \(P\) is left adjoint to the pushforward.
2819	
2820	  \emph{Step 3 (transport to the abstract pullback).} By definition
2821	  \(\mathtt{Scheme.Modules.pullback}\,f = (\mathtt{pushforward}\,f).\mathtt{leftAdjoint}\)
2822	  is \emph{a} left adjoint of the pushforward. By the uniqueness of left adjoints
2823	  \(\mathtt{Adjunction.leftAdjointUniq}\) --- the same device that closed
2824	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} --- there is a canonical natural isomorphism
2825	  \(\mathtt{Scheme.Modules.pullback}\,f \cong P\). The strong-monoidal structure of
2826	  \(P\) travels along this isomorphism, so its tensorator becomes a genuine
2827	  \emph{isomorphism}
2828	  \[
2829	    f^*(M \otimes_X N)
2830	      \;\xrightarrow{\;\sim\;}\;
2831	    (f^*M) \otimes_Y (f^*N),
2832	  \]
2833	  natural in \(M\) and \(N\). This is the asserted comparison; the abstract pullback is
2834	  never evaluated sectionwise --- the monoidal structure is transported across the
2835	  adjoint-uniqueness isomorphism.
2836	
2837	  The locus of the difficulty is precise. A presheaf-level oplax comparison
2838	  \emph{map} \(\delta\) already exists and costs nothing: the presheaf pushforward is lax
2839	  monoidal (\cref{lem:presheaf_pushforward_laxmonoidal}) and its left adjoint, the
2840	  presheaf pullback, is therefore oplax monoidal
2841	  (\cref{lem:presheaf_pullback_oplaxmonoidal}), via the bundled doctrinal adjunction
2842	  transfer \(\mathtt{Adjunction.leftAdjointOplaxMonoidal}\). The comparison \emph{map} \(\delta\) is thus free; the entire residual
2843	  difficulty is upgrading \(\delta\) to an \emph{isomorphism} for arbitrary \(M, N\).
2844	  This is what Step~3's transport along \(\mathtt{leftAdjointUniq}\) would deliver --- but
2845	  only once the concrete model \(P\) of Step~1 is constructed, and \(P\) requires the
2846	  Mathlib-absent presheaf extension of scalars and topological inverse image. There is no
2847	  shortcut from the bare oplax map: an (op)lax monoidal functor does \emph{not} preserve
2848	  invertible objects for free --- global sections \(\Gamma\) is lax monoidal yet
2849	  \(\Gamma(\mathbb{P}^1, \mathcal{O}(1)) = 0\) is not invertible --- so the iso must be
2850	  proved, not inferred from the doctrinal structure.
2851	
2852	  Because only the invertible case is needed, the substrate does not build \(P\) and does
2853	  not prove the general iso. Instead it works with the comparison map \(\delta_{\mathrm{sheaf}}\)
2854	  of \cref{lem:pullback_tensor_map} (the sheaf-level transport of the presheaf \(\delta\))
2855	  and proves it is an isomorphism on a single invertible pair by local trivialisation
2856	  (\cref{lem:isinvertible_pullback}), which never invokes \(P\) or the absent inverse
2857	  image.
2858	\end{proof}

2860	\begin{lemma}
2861	[The sheaf-level pullback--tensor comparison map $\delta_{\mathrm{sheaf}}$]
2862	  \label{lem:pullback_tensor_map}
2863	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.pullbackTensorMap}
2864	  \uses{lem:presheaf_pullback_oplaxmonoidal, def:scheme_modules_tensorobj,
2865	        lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}
2866	  Let \(f : Y \to X\) be a morphism of schemes and let
2867	  \(M, N \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) be \emph{arbitrary} \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules.
2868	  There is a canonical comparison \emph{morphism}
2869	  \[
2870	    \delta_{\mathrm{sheaf}} :
2871	      (\mathtt{Scheme.Modules.pullback}\,f).\mathrm{obj}\,(M \otimes_X N)
2872	      \;\longrightarrow\;
2873	      (\mathtt{Scheme.Modules.pullback}\,f).\mathrm{obj}\,M
2874	        \otimes_Y (\mathtt{Scheme.Modules.pullback}\,f).\mathrm{obj}\,N
2875	  \]
2876	  of \(\mathcal{O}_Y\)-modules, natural in \(M\) and \(N\). It is a \emph{map only}: in
2877	  general it is not asserted to be an isomorphism.
2878	\end{lemma}
2879	
2880	\begin{proof}
2881	  \uses{lem:presheaf_pullback_oplaxmonoidal, def:scheme_modules_tensorobj,
2882	        lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}
2883	  The presheaf pullback is oplax monoidal (\cref{lem:presheaf_pullback_oplaxmonoidal}),
2884	  so at the presheaf level it carries the comparison morphism
2885	  \[
2886	    \delta : (\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}\,(A \otimes B)
2887	      \;\longrightarrow\;
2888	      (\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}\,A
2889	        \otimes (\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}\,B,
2890	  \]
2891	  where \(\varphi\) is the ring-presheaf map presenting \(f\). One transports \(\delta\)
2892	  through sheafification to the sheaf level, using the same
2893	  \(\mathtt{sheafificationCompPullback}\) device that supplies Steps 1--2 of
2894	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}: the abstract pullback of \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules
2895	  agrees, up to canonical natural isomorphism, with the sheafification of the presheaf
2896	  pullback, and likewise the sheafified tensor product \(\otimes_Y\) is the
2897	  sheafification of the presheaf tensor. The right-hand side reconciliation of the unit
2898	  and tensor factors with their sheafified forms is supplied by the
2899	  sheafification-monoidality brick \(\mathtt{sheafifyTensorUnitIso}\). Whiskering and
2900	  composing these canonical isomorphisms with the sheafification of \(\delta\) produces
2901	  the asserted sheaf-level morphism \(\delta_{\mathrm{sheaf}}\), natural in \(M, N\)
2902	  because each ingredient is. No claim of invertibility is made: \(\delta_{\mathrm{sheaf}}\)
2903	  is the carrier on which the invertible-case isomorphism argument of
2904	  \cref{lem:isinvertible_pullback} acts.
2905	\end{proof}

2911	  \label{lem:unitToPushforwardObjUnit_comp}

2913	  Let \(h : Z \to Y\) and \(f : Y \to X\) be composable morphisms of schemes. Write

2915	  \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.unitToPushforwardObjUnit}\), the canonical map
2916	  \(\mathcal{O} \to f_*(\mathcal{O})\) comparing the structure sheaf of the target with
2917	  the pushforward of the structure sheaf of the source. Then \(\mathrm{upu}\) satisfies
2918	  the composition coherence
2919	  \[
2920	    \mathrm{upu}(h \mathbin{;} f)
2921	      \;=\;
2922	    \mathrm{upu}\,f \;\mathbin{;}\;
2923	      (\mathtt{pushforward}\,f).\mathrm{map}\bigl(\mathrm{upu}\,h\bigr) \;\mathbin{;}\;
2924	      (\mathtt{pushforwardComp}\,h\,f).\mathrm{hom},
2925	  \]
2926	  where \(\mathtt{pushforwardComp}\,h\,f : (h \mathbin{;} f)_* \cong f_* \circ h_*\) is the
2927	  pseudofunctoriality isomorphism of the pushforward. Sectionwise both sides are nothing
2928	  but the functoriality of the structure-sheaf ring maps, so the identity holds on the
2929	  nose after the comparison of sections. This is the right-adjoint half of the unit
2930	  composition coherence, and it is the input consumed by the mate transport that produces

2938	  pseudofunctor coherence \(\mathtt{pushforwardComp}\) acts as the identification of
2939	  \((h \mathbin{;} f)\)-pushforward sections with iterated \(f_*\,h_*\)-pushforward
2940	  sections. The composition coherence is therefore the functoriality identity
2941	  \((h \mathbin{;} f)^\sharp = h^\sharp \circ f^\sharp\) of the structure-sheaf ring maps,
2942	  which holds definitionally; the two sides agree section by section.
2943	\end{proof}

3115	  witness \(N\) and an isomorphism \(\psi : M \otimes_X N \xrightarrow{\sim}

3232	\section{The invertibility-carrier Picard group}
3233	\label{sec:tensorobj_pic_carrier}
3234	
3235	The group law of \cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup} is carried on the
3236	\emph{locally-trivial} predicate \(\mathtt{IsLocallyTrivial}\), and on that carrier
3237	the existence of a tensor inverse for each class is an extra obligation
3238	(\cref{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible}): one must \emph{manufacture} the dual
3239	object \(L^{-1} = \mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_X}(L, \mathcal{O}_X)\) and prove the
3240	contraction \(L \otimes_X L^{-1} \cong \mathcal{O}_X\), which is the whole content of
3241	the dual-infrastructure build of \cref{sec:tensorobj_dual_infra}.
3242	
3243	This section carries the group law on a \emph{different} predicate for which that
3244	obligation evaporates: the \(\otimes\)-invertibility predicate
3245	\(\mathtt{IsInvertible}\) of \cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible},
3246	\[
3247	  \mathtt{IsInvertible}(M)
3248	  \;:\equiv\;
3249	  \exists\, N,\ \mathrm{Nonempty}\bigl(M \otimes_X N \cong \mathcal{O}_X\bigr).
3250	\]
3251	On this carrier the inverse of a class \([M]\) is supplied by the \emph{membership
3252	witness itself} --- the very \(N\) whose existence \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}(M)\)
3253	asserts --- so no inverse object need be constructed and no separate
3254	tensor-inverse lemma is consumed. The cost is shifted to a routine
3255	well-definedness fact (\cref{lem:isinvertible_inverse_welldef}: the witness \(N\) is
3256	determined up to isomorphism), proved from the monoidal coherence isomorphisms
3257	alone. The carrier is the scheme-theoretic analogue of Mathlib's
3258	\(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\), whose elements are exactly the iso-classes of invertible
3259	modules (Stacks tag 01CX).
3260	
3261	\begin{lemma}
3262	
3263	\leanok
3264	[Associator for $\otimes_X$ on invertible objects (corollary of the unconditional
3265	associator)]
3266	  \label{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso_invertible}
3267	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_assoc_iso_invertible}
3268	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, def:scheme_modules_isinvertible,
3269	        lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}
3270	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and let
3271	  \(M, N, P \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) be \(\otimes\)-invertible
3272	  (\cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}). There is an isomorphism
3273	  \[
3274	    (M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X P
3275	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
3276	    M \otimes_X (N \otimes_X P)
3277	  \]
3278	  in \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). The invertibility-carrier group law
3279	  (\cref{thm:pic_commgroup}) consumes only the \emph{existence} of this
3280	  isomorphism, the proposition \(\mathrm{Nonempty}\bigl((M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X P
3281	  \cong M \otimes_X (N \otimes_X P)\bigr)\); no pentagon, triangle, or naturality
3282	  coherence is ever required.
3283	\end{lemma}
3284	
3285	\begin{proof}
3286	  \uses{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}
3287	  Immediate specialisation of the \emph{unconditional} associator
3288	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso} to the invertible arguments \(M, N, P\); no
3289	  hypothesis on the arguments is used. (The earlier route argued via ``invertible
3290	  \(\Rightarrow\) locally free \(\Rightarrow\) sectionwise flat'' to feed the
3291	  flat-whiskering lemma \cref{lem:flat_whisker_localizer}; that step is \emph{false}
3292	  --- local freeness does not give global sectionwise flatness \(M(U)\) over
3293	  \(\mathcal{O}_X(U)\) for a non-affine open \(U\) --- and is no longer needed, since

1	\chapter{Rigidity for morphisms of schemes (scheme-level form; Mumford \S4 in the abelian-variety case)}
2	\label{chap:Rigidity}
3	
4	This chapter records a self-contained helper: \emph{rigidity for morphisms of schemes that agree on a non-empty open}. The classical motivation is Mumford's rigidity for abelian-variety morphisms, but the project's formalised statement is more general: the source-side group-object hypothesis is unused by the proof and is dropped.
5	
6	The lemma is independent of the construction of \(\Jac(C)\). It is the load-bearing input for the M2.a sub-step of the genus-0 Albanese-witness argument (Jacobian.tex \S C.2.b), where the source is taken to be \(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\) (which is \emph{not} a group scheme).
7	
8	\section{Statement}
9	
10	\begin{theorem}\leanok
11	[Rigidity for morphisms of schemes (scheme-level form)]
12	  \label{thm:GrpObj_eq_of_eqOnOpen}
13	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Over.ext_of_eqOnOpen}
14	  Let \(k\) be a field. Let \(X\) be a reduced geometrically irreducible \(k\)-scheme and let \(Y\) be a separated \(k\)-scheme. Let \(g_1, g_2 \colon X \to Y\) be two morphisms of \(k\)-schemes, and let \(U \subseteq X\) be a non-empty open subset. If
15	  \[
16	    U.\iota \circ g_1 \;=\; U.\iota \circ g_2
17	  \]
18	  as scheme morphisms (where \(U.\iota \colon U \hookrightarrow X\) is the canonical open immersion), then \(g_1 = g_2\) as morphisms of \(k\)-schemes.
19	\end{theorem}
20	
21	\begin{remark}
22	  The classical Mumford rigidity theorem (\emph{Abelian Varieties}, \S4) usually states the special case where \(Y\) is an abelian variety and the conclusion is phrased as ``a morphism vanishing on a non-empty open is zero''. The two forms are interderivable in the abelian-variety case via the difference morphism \(\delta = g_1 \cdot g_2^{-1}\) formed from the group law on \(Y\). The project formalises the more general statement above because the M2.a (genus-0 Albanese-witness) consumer specifically needs the source to be \(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\), which is not a group scheme: the abelian-variety packaging is consumer-side and the scheme-level statement is what the proof actually establishes.
23	
24	  The refactor that landed the scheme-level form (iter-125; see \texttt{analogies/rigidity-refactor.md}) dropped 8 unused hypotheses from the project's original group-scheme-flavoured declaration and weakened the target-side \([\mathtt{IsProper}\ Y.\mathrm{hom}]\) to \([\mathtt{IsSeparated}\ Y.\mathrm{hom}]\).
25	\end{remark}
26	
27	\section{Proof sketch}
28	
29	\begin{proof}
30	  \leanok
31	  We reduce to Mathlib's \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.ext\_of\_isDominant\_of\_isSeparated'} (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Morphisms.Separated}), which gives the standard scheme-level fact that two morphisms from a reduced scheme to a separated scheme agreeing on a dominant subscheme agree everywhere.
32	
33	  The three load-bearing instances feeding that closure are:
34	  \begin{itemize}
35	    \item \emph{\(Y \to \Spec k\) is separated.} Hypothesis \([\mathtt{IsSeparated}\ Y.\mathrm{hom}]\) provides this directly (no projection from \(\mathtt{IsProper}\) needed). The \texttt{OverClass.fromOver} instance transports it to the form \(\mathtt{IsSeparated}\ (Y.\mathrm{left} \downarrow \Spec k)\) that the closure step expects.
36	    \item \emph{\(X\) is an irreducible space.} Hypothesis \([\mathtt{GeometricallyIrreducible}\ X.\mathrm{hom}]\) over the field-spectrum base \(\Spec k\) (which is a single point, hence subsingleton) yields \(\mathtt{IrreducibleSpace}\ X.\mathrm{left}\) via \texttt{GeometricallyIrreducible.irreducibleSpace\_of\_subsingleton}.
37	    \item \emph{The inclusion \(U.\iota\) is dominant.} A non-empty open in an irreducible space is dense, and a dense open immersion is dominant: \texttt{Scheme.PartialMap.Opens.isDominant\_ι}.
38	  \end{itemize}
39	  With these three derived instances and the hypothesis \([\mathtt{IsReduced}\ X.\mathrm{left}]\), the closure \texttt{ext\_of\_isDominant\_of\_isSeparated'} produces the underlying scheme-morphism equality \(g_1.\mathrm{left} = g_2.\mathrm{left}\) from the agreement-on-\(U\) hypothesis. The promotion to \(g_1 = g_2\) in \(\mathrm{Over}\ (\Spec k)\) uses \texttt{Over.OverMorphism.ext}.
40	
41	  \medskip
42	  \noindent\textbf{Mathlib ingredients.}
43	  \begin{itemize}
44	    \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.ext\_of\_isDominant\_of\_isSeparated'} for the scheme-level closure step.
45	    \item \texttt{GeometricallyIrreducible.irreducibleSpace\_of\_subsingleton} to derive irreducibility of \(X.\mathrm{left}\).
46	    \item \texttt{Scheme.PartialMap.Opens.isDominant\_ι} to derive dominance of the open immersion \(U.\iota\) from the non-empty hypothesis.
47	    \item \texttt{Over.OverMorphism.ext} to promote underlying scheme equality to \(\mathrm{Over}\)-morphism equality.
48	    \item No abelian-variety / group-object infrastructure is needed: smoothness, properness, and group-object hypotheses on \(X\) and \(Y\) are all dropped from the formalised statement.
49	  \end{itemize}
50	\end{proof}
51	
52	\section{Use in the project}
53	
54	The rigidity theorem is used in two places downstream:
55	
56	\begin{itemize}
57	  \item \textbf{M2.a (genus-0 Albanese witness).} Jacobian.tex \S C.2.b reduces the genus-0 rigidity-over-\(\bar k\) argument (for \(f \colon \mathbb P^1_{\bar k} \to A_{\bar k}\) hitting the identity at a \(\bar k\)-point) to this theorem with \(X := \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\) and \(Y := A_{\bar k}\). The refactor of iter-125 (dropping the source-side group-object hypothesis) is what enables this application. The named project declaration packaging the C.2.a–C.2.e reduction is \cref{thm:rigidity_over_kbar} in \cref{chap:RigidityKbar}; that chapter scaffolds the iter-126 Lean target \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.rigidity\_over\_kbar} with the C.2.d cotangent-vanishing keystone residual.
58	  \item \textbf{Uniqueness in the Albanese property.} Used in the uniqueness half of Theorem~REF (the Albanese property of \(\Jac(C)\)): given two morphisms \(g_1, g_2 \colon \Jac(C) \to A\) with \(g_1 \circ \alpha_P = g_2 \circ \alpha_P\), scheme-level agreement on the image of \(\alpha_P\) (a non-empty open after passing to \(C^{(g)} \twoheadrightarrow \Jac(C)\)) forces \(g_1 = g_2\).
59	\end{itemize}
60	
61	Note: the existence half of REF requires Phase~B/C infrastructure (Picard scheme, dual abelian variety). Rigidity alone yields \emph{uniqueness} only.
62	
63	\section{Mathlib status}
64	
65	The scheme-level form is a thin specialization of Mathlib's existing \texttt{ext\_of\_isDominant\_of\_isSeparated'} (in \texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Morphisms.Separated}); the project's contribution is the \(\mathrm{Over}\ (\Spec k)\)-bundled packaging plus the convenience derivations of \texttt{IrreducibleSpace} and \texttt{IsDominant} from the project-side hypotheses.
66

1	\chapter{Genus of a smooth proper curve}
2	\label{chap:Genus}
3	
4	Throughout this chapter, \(k\) denotes a field and \(C\) a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over \(k\) of relative dimension \(1\). In other words, \(C \to \Spec k\) is a morphism of schemes carrying the geometric typeclasses that make it a curve in the modern sense.
5	
6	\section{Definition}
7	
8	% NOTE: classical definition \(g(C) = \dim_k H^1(C, \mathcal O_C)\); see
9	% Hartshorne, *Algebraic Geometry*, Ch.~IV \S1 (genus of a curve) — TOC
10	% summarised in `references/hartshorne-ag.md`. The finiteness backing
11	% (Serre on coherent cohomology of a proper \(k\)-scheme) is queued in
12	% `references/stacks-0BUG.md` and chapter
13	% \texttt{Cohomology\_StructureSheafModuleK}.
14	\begin{definition}\leanok
15	[Genus of a smooth proper curve]
16	  \label{def:genus}
17	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.genus}
18	  
19	  The \emph{genus} of a smooth proper curve \(C\) over \(k\) is the natural number
20	  \[
21	    g(C) \;=\; \dim_k H^1(C,\, \mathcal O_C),
22	  \]
23	  the \(k\)-dimension of the first sheaf-cohomology group of the structure sheaf of \(C\).
24	\end{definition}
25	
26	\begin{proof}
27	  
28	  \leanok
29	  The definition is realised by passing the structure sheaf through the sheaf-of-\(k\)-modules construction and applying the \(k\)-linear sheaf cohomology \(H^1\). The result is a \(k\)-vector space; its dimension is extracted via the standard finiteness rank.
30	
31	  \begin{remark}[Lean implementation]
32	    The formal definition uses the project's \(\ModuleCat k\)-flavoured sheaf cohomology of the structure sheaf:
33	    \[
34	      \mathtt{genus}\,C \;:=\; \Module.\mathtt{finrank}\,k\,\bigl(\Scheme.\HModule\,k\,(\Scheme.\toModuleKSheaf\,C)\,1\bigr).
35	    \]
36	    The carrier \(\toModuleKSheaf C\) is the sheaf-of-\(k\)-modules constructed from \(\struct C\) via the structure morphism; the wrapper \(\HModule k F 1\) is the \(\Module k\)-flavoured sheaf cohomology, given by \(\Ext\) from the constant sheaf at \(\ModuleCat.\mathrm{of}\,k\,k\), which carries a canonical \(\Module k\)-structure inherited from the \(\Linear k\)-enrichment of the sheaf category.
37	  \end{remark}
38	
39	  Mathlib's \(\Module.\mathtt{finrank}\) returns the \(k\)-dimension when the underlying module is finite-dimensional and zero otherwise. For a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve, Serre's theorem on coherent cohomology of a proper \(k\)-scheme guarantees \(\dim_k H^1(C, \struct C) < \infty\), so \(g(C)\) is the geometric genus on the relevant case. The closure is therefore the one-liner above, confirmed to typecheck against current Mathlib.
40	\end{proof}
41	
42	Equivalent reformulations available once Riemann--Roch and Serre duality are formalised:
43	\begin{itemize}
44	  \item \(g(C) = \dim_k H^0(C,\, \Omega^1_{C/k})\) (Serre duality).
45	  \item \(g(C) = 1 - \chi(\mathcal O_C)\), where \(\chi\) is the Euler characteristic.
46	  \item For a smooth projective curve over \(\mathbb C\), \(g(C)\) equals the topological genus of \(C(\mathbb C)\).
47	\end{itemize}
48	Each of these requires Mathlib infrastructure that is currently absent. The first reformulation in particular requires the sheaf of relative differentials of schemes (Phase~B), and the others require Riemann--Roch / Serre duality / a topological-vs-algebraic geometry comparison.
49	
50	\section{Mathlib gap}
51	\label{sec:Genus_status}
52	
53	The honest definition \(\dim_k H^1(C, \mathcal O_C)\) requires a \(\Module k\)-flavoured sheaf cohomology of \(\struct C\) on the topology of opens of the underlying space of \(C\). Through earlier iterations the project has built this:
54	
55	\begin{itemize}
56	  \item \textbf{Sheaf cohomology of \(\AddCommGrpCat\)-valued sheaves} (Mathlib): defined as \(\Ext\) from the constant sheaf at \(\AddCommGrpCat.\mathrm{of}\,(\ULift\,\mathbb Z)\).
57	  \item \textbf{Sheaf cohomology of \(\ModuleCat k\)-valued sheaves} (this project): the parallel \(\Module k\)-valued construction with unit object \(\ModuleCat.\mathrm{of}\,k\,k\) instead of \(\ULift\,\mathbb Z\), gluing through the \(\Linear k\)-enrichment of the sheaf category to recover a \(\Module k\) structure on the cohomology groups.
58	  \item \textbf{Structure sheaf as a \(\ModuleCat k\)-valued sheaf} (this project): for \(C\) over \(\Spec k\), the sheafification of the presheaf \(U \mapsto \ModuleCat.\mathrm{of}\,k\,(\Gamma(C, U))\) where the \(k\)-action is the algebra structure induced by the structure morphism.
59	  \item \textbf{\(H^0\) algebraic bridge} (this project): \(H^0(C, \struct C)_{\Module k} \simeq_k \Hom_{\Sheaf}(\mathrm{const}, \toModuleKSheaf C)\), via Mathlib's \(\Ext\) linear equivalence. Used downstream for the geometric content \(H^0(C, \struct C) \simeq k\) on a connected proper \(k\)-curve.
60	\end{itemize}

1	\chapter{Mayer--Vietoris long exact sequence for sheaf cohomology with \(k\)-module coefficients}
2	\label{chap:Cohomology_MayerVietoris}
3	
4	This chapter constructs the Mayer--Vietoris long exact sequence for sheaf cohomology valued in \(k\)-modules, and develops the \v{C}ech-acyclicity machinery for affine basic-open covers. Together these provide the categorical engine for the Serre finiteness argument (Phase~A step~6). The carriers \(H^n(X,\mathcal F)\) and \(H^{\prime n}(U,\mathcal F)\) live in Chapter~REF and are imported here.
5	
6	The chapter introduces no protected declarations: every definition, lemma, and instance here is auxiliary, supporting the construction of \(H^1(C,\mathcal O_C)\) in Definition~REF.
7	
8	\section{Functoriality of cohomology in the open variable}
9	\label{sec:hmodule_prime_cohomologyPresheaf}
10	
11	The cohomology-of-an-open carrier \(H^{\prime n}(X,\mathcal F)\) is contravariantly functorial in the open variable \(X\). Assembling this functoriality yields a presheaf
12	\[
13	  X \longmapsto H^{\prime n}(X, \mathcal F),
14	\]
15	and, varying \(\mathcal F\) as well, a bifunctor from sheaves of \(k\)-modules to presheaves of abelian groups. The codomain is the category of abelian groups because \(\Ext\) is always valued there, regardless of the linear enrichment of the source category; the per-fiber \(k\)-module structure is inherited from the reducible wrapper on \(H^{\prime n}\).
16	
17	\begin{definition}\leanok
18	[Cohomology presheaf functor, \(H'\) flavor]
19	  \label{def:Scheme_HModule_prime_cohomologyPresheafFunctor}
20	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.HModule'_cohomologyPresheafFunctor}
21	  
22	  Let \(k\) be a field, \(C\) a category equipped with a Grothendieck topology \(J\), and assume sheafification and \(\Ext\) exist for sheaves of \(k\)-modules on \(J\). For \(n \in \mathbb N\), define the cohomology presheaf functor
23	  \[
24	    \mathcal H^{\prime n}(k, J) \;:\; \Sheaf\,J\,(\ModuleCat\,k) \longrightarrow (C^{\op} \longrightarrow \AddCommGrpCat)
25	  \]
26	  by the standard composition of the Yoneda embedding, the free-\(k\)-module functor, sheafification, and the \(n\)-th \(\Ext\) functor.
27	\end{definition}
28	
29	\begin{proof}
30	  
31	  \leanok
32	  The body is a direct categorical composition mirroring the general sheaf-cohomology presheaf functor. Universe pinning is required because the morphism universe of the sheaf category lives one level above the object universe.
33	\end{proof}
34	
35	\begin{definition}\leanok
36	[Cohomology presheaf, \(H'\) flavor]
37	  \label{def:Scheme_HModule_prime_cohomologyPresheaf}
38	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.HModule'_cohomologyPresheaf}
39	  
40	  In the setting of Definition~REF, let \(\mathcal F\) be a sheaf of \(k\)-modules. For \(n \in \mathbb N\), define the cohomology presheaf
41	  \[
42	    \mathcal H^{\prime n}(\mathcal F) \;:\; C^{\op} \longrightarrow \AddCommGrpCat
43	  \]
44	  to be the evaluation of the bifunctor at \(\mathcal F\).
45	\end{definition}
46	
47	\begin{proof}
48	  
49	  \leanok
50	  Direct evaluation of the bifunctor at \(\mathcal F\).
51	\end{proof}
52	
53	\begin{remark}
54	  For every object \(X\) of \(C\), the underlying type of \(\mathcal H^{\prime n}(\mathcal F)(X)\) is definitionally equal to \(H^{\prime n}(X, \mathcal F)\). The per-fiber \(k\)-module structure is therefore inherited through the \(H'\) wrapper.
55	\end{remark}
56	
57	\begin{remark}[Lean implementation]
58	  The cohomology presheaf functor is declared as a non-computable definition and the presheaf as a non-computable abbrev, matching the reducibility conventions of the general sheaf-cohomology API. The abbrev form is load-bearing: downstream definitional unfolding needs to see through to the fiber-level \(H'\) carrier.
59	\end{remark}
60
